questions.question: What best describes a foundation model?

answers.text: A large model pre-trained on broad data that can be adapted to many tasks
A small model trained for one narrow task
A rules engine that follows hand-written instructions

questions.feedback: Foundation models are broadly pre-trained and reusable across many tasks, which is why one model can summarise, chat, translate, and more.

questions.question: A model confidently produces an answer that sounds right but is factually made up. What is this called?
questions.feedback: Hallucination is confident but incorrect output. It's the headline generative-AI risk; ground answers with RAG and review high-stakes output.

questions.question: A team wants to build a chatbot using a choice of foundation models via one API, with no infrastructure to manage. Which service?
questions.feedback: Bedrock offers many foundation models behind one managed, serverless API. Rekognition (vision) and Textract (documents) are unrelated tasks.

questions.question: Which are genuine limitations of generative AI to keep in mind?

answers.text: It can hallucinate, producing confident but wrong output
A base model doesn't know recent events or your private data unless you supply them
The same prompt can produce different answers (nondeterminism)
It guarantees a single exact, verifiable answer every time

questions.feedback: Hallucination, knowledge cutoff, and nondeterminism are real limits. Guaranteed exact answers are exactly what generative AI does NOT provide without safeguards.
